title: Math 314: Final Exam In-Class Practice
description: Practice exam covering eigenvalues, multiplicities, symmetric matrices, and orthogonal transformations. All LaTeX is KaTeX-compatible.

questions.prompt: Find all eigenvalues and compute their algebraic and geometric multiplicities for $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: The eigenvalues are the diagonal entries 2 and 3. For each, the geometric multiplicity is the number of Jordan blocks, which is 1.

questions.prompt: Give an example of a $2\times 2$ matrix that is invertible but not diagonalizable.
questions.explanation: This matrix has $\det=1$ (invertible) but only one eigenvector (not diagonalizable).

questions.prompt: Give an example of a $2\times 2$ matrix that is diagonalizable but not invertible.
questions.explanation: This is a diagonal matrix (diagonalizable) with determinant 0 (not invertible).

questions.prompt: True or False: For every matrix $X$ and every vector $y$, there is always a solution $w$ to $Xw=y$.

choices.label: False — only if $y$ is in the column space of $X$

questions.explanation: The equation $Xw=y$ is consistent only if $y \in \text{Col}(X)$.

questions.prompt: True or False: There is a $4 \times 32$ matrix $A$ such that $A^T A$ has characteristic polynomial $\lambda^2(\lambda+3)^{30}$.

choices.label: False — eigenvalues of $A^T A$ must be $\ge 0$

questions.explanation: $A^T A$ is positive semi-definite; all its eigenvalues are $\ge 0$. A factor of $(\lambda+3)^{30}$ implies $\lambda=-3$, which is impossible.

questions.prompt: True or False: There is a $3 \times 3$ matrix with real entries that has no real eigenvalues.

choices.label: False — odd-degree real polynomials always have a real root

questions.explanation: Cubic polynomials with real coefficients must intersect the x-axis at least once.

questions.prompt: True or False: If $AA^T = I_n$, then the rows of $A$ form an orthonormal basis for $\mathbb{R}^n$.
questions.explanation: $AA^T = I$ means that the dot product of row $i$ with row $j$ is $\delta_{ij}$, which defines an orthonormal set.

choices.label: Yes — it has 3 linearly independent eigenvectors

questions.explanation: The equations define three eigenvectors for eigenvalues 2 and 7. Since these three vectors are linearly independent, $A$ is diagonalizable.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be a symmetric $3 \times 3$ matrix with eigenvalues 0 and 3. If 0 is an eigenvalue, what is the determinant of $A$?
questions.explanation: The determinant is the product of the eigenvalues.

questions.prompt: Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. Which is true about the diagonalizability of $A$?

choices.label: $A$ is diagonalizable because it has 3 distinct eigenvalues
$A$ is not diagonalizable because it is upper triangular
$A$ is only diagonalizable if it is symmetric

questions.explanation: A $3 \times 3$ matrix with 3 distinct eigenvalues is always diagonalizable.
